attributes.stem:
A 31-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with an 18-hour history of vaginal bleeding and mild abdominal cramping. She is 8 weeks pregnant and had an ultrasound with her GP one week ago which showed a viable intrauterine pregnancy with a fetal heart beat. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Speculum examination demonstrates an open os, with moderate pooling of blood in the posterior fornix. Products of conception are visible in the cervical canal and are removed by the emergency doctor. Following this, the bleeding slows significantly. Bimanual examination demonstrates a soft and central uterus approximately 7/40 size. Ultrasound after removal of the products shows possible retained products of conception (22×12×15mm).

She lives 10 minutes from the local tertiary hospital and has a supportive family network.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management option for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with an incomplete miscarriage, with products of conception having been removed from the cervical canal by the emergency doctor. Ultrasound shows possible retained products of conception. The patient is hemodynamically stable with slowed bleeding after removal of visible products.

Expectant, medical, or surgical management are all appropriate options for this patient. The choice between these approaches should be based on patient preference after thorough counseling about the risks, benefits, and expected outcomes of each approach. Given that the patient is hemodynamically stable, with slowed bleeding, and has good access to healthcare facilities (living 10 minutes from a tertiary hospital), she is a suitable candidate for any of these management strategies.

Expectant management only would involve allowing the remaining products of conception to pass naturally without intervention. While this is a reasonable option for this patient, presenting it as the only option fails to acknowledge that other approaches may be equally valid based on patient preference and circumstances.

Medical management only would typically involve administration of misoprostol to facilitate expulsion of any remaining products. This is also a reasonable option but should not be presented as the only choice. Medical management has success rates of approximately 80-90% for incomplete miscarriage.

Surgical management only would involve dilation and curettage (D&C) to remove any remaining products of conception. While this provides the most definitive and immediate resolution, it carries surgical risks and may be unnecessary if the patient prefers a less invasive approach. However, it remains an appropriate option that should be discussed.

Immediate hospital admission for observation is not necessary for a hemodynamically stable patient with slowed bleeding who lives close to hospital facilities. This would be an overutilization of resources when outpatient management is appropriate.

Regardless of the management approach chosen, appropriate follow-up should be arranged to ensure complete resolution of the miscarriage. This may include serial beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements, repeat ultrasound, or clinical assessment. Additionally, all patients should receive emotional support and counseling regarding the loss of pregnancy.
learningPoints: Expectant, medical, and surgical management are all appropriate options for a patient with an incomplete miscarriage who is hemodynamically stable.
Patient preference, clinical circumstances, and resource availability should guide the choice of management for incomplete miscarriage.
Follow-up is essential regardless of the management approach chosen for incomplete miscarriage.
references: Doubilet P, Benson C, Bourne T, Blaivas M. Diagnostic Criteria for Nonviable Pregnancy Early in the First Trimester. New England Journal of Medicine. 2014;30(1):3-9.

Tulandi T. Ectopic pregnancy: choosing a treatment. In: Scheiber C A, Chakrabarti A, editors. UpToDate [Internet]. Waltham (MA): UpToDate Inc, September 10 2021 [Cited: January 12 2022]. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com.acs.hcn.com.au/contents/ectopic-pregnancy-choosing-a-treatment?search=ectopic%20pregnancy&source=search_result&selectedTitle=3~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=3#H3021183296

Dilatation and curettage (D&C) - Better Health Channel [Internet]. Betterhealth.vic.gov.au. 2021 [cited 12 January 2022]. Available from: https://www.betterhealth.vic.gov.au/health/conditionsandtreatments/dilatation-and-curettage-dc
